focus: financial data / screening tools / investment research

strategies.name: Freemium Tool + Opt-Out Trial Paywall
strategies.description: Offer a genuinely useful free tier (limited stocks, delayed data, basic filters) to build an audience, then gate high-value features behind a subscription accessed via an opt-out trial (credit card required upfront). The free tool is the acquisition engine; the trial is the conversion engine.
example_products: TIKR Terminal (free US data → Plus $19.95/mo → Pro $39.95/mo)
conversion_tactics: Require credit card at trial start (opt-out) — converts at ~50% vs 18% opt-in, vs 3–4% pure freemium
Gate the most-wanted features: real-time data, data export/CSV, alerts, extended financial history, API access
Show a 'preview blur' of locked data so users can see value before paying
Limit free tier to 5–10 results per screen or 1-day delayed data to create natural friction
Surface upgrade prompt at the moment of highest intent (e.g. user tries to export or set an alert)
Use 7-day trial — data shows 40.4% conversion rate vs 30.6% for 60+ day trials
strategies.typical_price_points: $19–$79/month; annual plans at 35–40% discount (e.g. $25/mo billed annually) to boost LTV
strategies.why_it_works: Financial tool users have high intent — they are researching where to put real money. Once they find a workflow that works, switching cost is high. The free tier builds habit and trust; the paywall captures willingness-to-pay at peak engagement. Opt-out trials pre-qualify users with payment intent, cutting churn from the start.

strategies.name: Tiered Annual Subscriptions with Feature-Gated Data Depth
strategies.description: Structure 2–3 subscription tiers that progressively unlock data depth, coverage breadth, and professional features. Push hard for annual billing via steep monthly-vs-annual price anchoring. This is the core monetisation engine of nearly every profitable financial data SaaS.
example_products: Simply Wall St (Starter → Unlimited → Premium plans)
Financial Modeling Prep API (Free → Starter $14 → Professional $49 → Enterprise $99/mo)
conversion_tactics: Annual plan anchoring: show monthly price crossed out, highlight annual savings ('Save 37%') — annual plans cut churn by 30% and lift LTV by 27%
3-tier structure: Free (lead gen), Mid ($20–$40/mo, core users), Pro ($60–$120/mo, power users / advisors)
Lock export, API access, portfolio tracking, and alerts exclusively to paid tiers
Display plan comparison table with checkmarks — make the free tier look obviously limited
Offer a 'Founder/Early Bird' annual plan at launch to generate upfront cash and lock in churn-resistant users
Email drip campaign at day 3, 7, 14 of free use highlighting features they haven't tried yet that are behind the paywall
strategies.typical_price_points: Mid tier: $19–$49/mo (or $15–$35/mo billed annually). Pro tier: $79–$299/mo. Annual prepay generates 10–12x monthly upfront cash.
strategies.why_it_works: Investors and traders are repeat, habitual users — daily or weekly workflows. Annual billing aligns with their investment horizon mindset. Tiering lets solo founders serve both retail traders ($20/mo) and RIAs/advisors ($200/mo) from the same product. Median profitable micro-SaaS in this model earns ~$4.2K MRR with 95% reaching profitability in year one.

strategies.name: Paid One-Time Reports + Data Deliverables (Product-Led Monetisation)
strategies.description: Sell high-value, one-time downloadable research reports, screener outputs, or curated data exports alongside (or instead of) a subscription. The screening tool or newsletter acts as a trust-building funnel; the paid report is a low-friction, high-margin transaction. This is the fastest path to first revenue for a solo founder with a working product.
example_products: eMarketer (free article summaries → paid full reports at $1,500–$4,000+)
Substack finance newsletters (free posts → paid tier $10–$30/mo or one-off report PDFs at $29–$99)
Statista (free basic data → premium reports / dataset downloads at $129–$2,990)
Independent ASX/small-cap screener reports sold via Gumroad or Stripe ($29–$149/report)
conversion_tactics: Publish a free teaser version of the report (top 3 companies, methodology overview) — full list and data behind a paywall
Use scarcity: 'Updated monthly — next edition in 14 days' creates urgency to buy current edition
Price anchor against Bloomberg/Refinitiv ($500+/mo) — a $49 report feels trivially cheap by comparison
Offer a bundle: 3-month report subscription at a discount vs per-report price
Post results snippets on social media (Bluesky, X, LinkedIn) with CTA to buy full report — builds social proof and organic discovery
Email list is the asset: capture emails with free tier, convert with report launch announcements
Stripe/Gumroad one-click checkout removes all friction — no account creation required for first purchase
strategies.typical_price_points: Single report: $29–$149. Monthly report subscription: $19–$49/mo. Annual data subscription: $199–$499/yr. API data access: $99–$299/mo.
strategies.why_it_works: One-time purchases have zero churn risk and near-zero CAC when distribution is organic (social + SEO). A solo founder can validate monetisation with a single Gumroad link before building any subscription infrastructure. Reports also compound — a library of past reports can be sold as an archive bundle. For niche markets (e.g. ASX small-caps), there is minimal competition and buyers are self-selecting professionals willing to pay for edge.

key_takeaways: Opt-out trials (card required upfront) are the single highest-converting tactic: ~50% conversion vs 3–4% for pure freemium — use this as the default gate once product-market fit is proven
Annual billing is a retention superweapon: 30% lower churn, 27% higher LTV — anchor all pricing around annual plans with a prominent discount
For a solo founder with a working product, start monetisation with one-time paid reports (zero infrastructure, zero churn) then migrate proven buyers into subscriptions
Gate the 3 things financial users want most: data export/CSV, real-time (vs delayed) data, and alerts — never give these away free
Social distribution (organic posts with data snippets + CTA) is the cheapest acquisition channel — CAC of near $0, compounds over time
Niche specificity is a pricing moat: an ASX small-cap screener has less competition than a generic global screener and buyers expect to pay more for specialised edge
The freemium-to-paid conversion funnel depends on showing premium value before the gate — blur/preview locked data rather than hiding it entirely
7-day opt-out trial outperforms both longer trials and freemium: short enough to create urgency, long enough to prove value
